EP.20 Learning JS From Zero — Day 20: Getting to Know the BOM
Day 20 of the reincarnation. This time we're going to look at the BOM.

BOM stands for Browser Object Model. So what's it actually for? First off, there's no hard official standard for the BOM, so browsers are free to implement it however they like — but in practice most browsers end up with a pretty similar BOM. If the DOM is what you use to control HTML elements, then the BOM is what you use to control the browser itself. When you open a browser, it creates this thing called
is the whole browser window — the DOM lives inside it too.
Because of that, we can use methods on
to control the BOM — stuff like jumping to another page, going back, going forward, or grabbing the screen size. And yeah, popup windows are a kind of BOM thing too.
So to sum it up: the DOM operates on the page, and the BOM operates on the browser. With that out of the way, let's note down some of the common BOM operations.
So which BOM operations come up the most? Mainly these:
history - go back and forward between pages
screen - get the screen height and width
outerHeight - get the browser window height
close - close the current browser window (not used much these days)
So let's walk through how to actually write each one.

Day 20 of my reincarnated JavaScript journey. This time we're looking at the BOM (Browser Object Model) — what it is, how it differs from the DOM, and the most common BOM operations you'll actually reach for, like history, location, screen, navigator, and print.
